comments.comment_body:
When I see my own code too after some time too 😂

I think it's because we don't have context so it's mentally hard to read code we haven't recently written. 

Most of the time there is a reason things are the way they are and after some time working on the product I come to rediscover it.
comments.post_body:
Some Thoughts on Development

As a developer, I care a lot about the scalability of the systems I build and like to think long-term.

But that’s not always the case. Sometimes, I realize I just need to kick it off and get it done. There are times when I don’t need to over-engineer the code or create long-term solutions. Real-world problems can be quite different from ideal system designs.

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the importance of balancing scalability and timely delivery in software development, highlighting that sometimes quick solutions are necessary, especially in proof of concept projects. The commenter is responding to the post's thoughts on development and the need to adapt to real-world problems.

comments.comment_body: Frank Li spot on！

comments.post_body:
Hi everyone,

Since I am looking for work, I decided to share a weekly status report with my potential future co-workers. I hope you are all doing well and had a great week.

Progress:

- Updated my resume and re-hosted it using AWS S3 + CloudFront. I finally completed this long overdue task, and I'm happy with how it looks and how fast it loads.
- Started my job search and found several promising leads. I applied to some interesting positions and reached out to some former colleagues and friends. Thank you to all who replied and reposted my availability to their network.
- Migrated my personal app store apps site to S3 and CloudFront hosting. This was another task that I had been meaning to do for a while, and I wanted to improve the performance and security of my site and reduce the hosting costs. I used my print tool to publish changes.
- I finally shut down my Digital Ocean Droplets.
- Updated my open-source tool to publish static websites on AWS S3+CloudFront called "print." I added some new features and fixed some bugs. I also pushed two PRs to my project and opened some issues for future work. You can find the latest version here: https://lnkd.in/e_8K_pvD
- I signed up for Pluralsight and am enjoying the content there to catch up on the latest technologies. I decided to invest in my professional development and learn some new skills. I bookmarked courses on Android, Azure, PowerShell, and C#. So far, I'm enjoying the content and the quizzes.
- Made progress on re-writing my web page to React. I wanted to update my web page with a more modern and interactive design. I chose React as the framework because I want to learn more by doing. I'm still working on the layout and the components.
- Making progress on the Spring lawn tasks. I managed to weed the flower beds and spread mulch in the front yard before the rain.

Plans:

- Continue my job search and follow up with potential employers. I hope to schedule some interviews next week or the week after.
- Finish re-writing my web page in React and deploy it to S3 and CloudFront Hosting.
- Complete two Pluralsight courses.
- Finish the Spring lawn tasks left in the backyard.

That's all for this week. Thank you for reading my status report. Please let me know if you have any questions, comments, or feedback, and keep referring me to folks looking for talented developers.

Have a great weekend!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is sharing their weekly status report as they search for work, highlighting their progress on various personal projects and job search efforts, including updating their resume and applying to promising leads. They also outline their plans for the upcoming week, including continuing their job search and following up with potential employers.

comments.post_body:
Attention all friends and fellow engineers! I'm reaching out to you today because I'm in dire need of your help. Unfortunately, some truly exceptional engineers were let go from our team this week. These colleagues have been instrumental in my growth and development within the company, and I've learned so much from them

That's why I'm reaching out to you, my trusted network. I was hoping that perhaps someone out there might be able to offer a referral for these amazing engineers. They are not only incredibly talented, but also wonderful individuals to work with. They have a passion for their craft that is unmatched, and their expertise has made a significant impact on our team.

So, if you or someone you know is looking to hire talented and driven engineers, please don't hesitate to reach out. I would be forever grateful for your help in finding a new home for these amazing individuals. Together, we can help them land on their feet and continue to thrive in their careers. Thank you in advance for your help!

Some additional info:
- These engineers are based in Vancouver or in the US
- Focused more on backend development though have full stack experience

Edit: Thanks for all the help everyone! Please keep them coming! 🚀
comments.context_summary: The author is seeking help from their network to find new job opportunities for exceptional engineers who were recently let go from their team, describing them as talented, driven, and passionate about their craft. The engineers are based in Vancouver or the US, with a focus on backend development and full stack experience.

comments.comment_body: I think a child component should have its own unit tests because the reason you have a child component is that it can be reused elsewhere and be changed independently from the parent.
comments.post_body:
A button has its own test cases. Now, if that button is used inside another component, is it necessary to test that same button while writing that component's tests also? 
Should we skip testing that button at that level? Or should we limit the test cases for that button?

P.S. No right or wrong answers here

#testing #writing #opinion #poll #webdevelopment
comments.context_summary: The commenter is discussing whether it's necessary to re-test a button's functionality when it's used within another component, and seeking opinions on the best approach to testing in this scenario. The goal is to determine if testing the button at the component level is redundant or if it should be limited.

comments.comment_body: That is true, but the question is, do we need to test that button again if it is used in some bigger component?
comments.post_body:
A button has its own test cases. Now, if that button is used inside another component, is it necessary to test that same button while writing that component's tests also? 
Should we skip testing that button at that level? Or should we limit the test cases for that button?

P.S. No right or wrong answers here

#testing #writing #opinion #poll #webdevelopment
comments.parent_comment_body: I think a child component should have its own unit tests because the reason you have a child component is that it can be reused elsewhere and be changed independently from the parent.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is discussing the necessity of testing a button that is used inside another component, and is responding to the idea that child components should have their own unit tests for reusability and independence. The commenter is weighing the pros and cons of testing the button at multiple levels.

comments.comment_body:
I would say it depends on how you treat that button. If you consider the button component as a library and you fully trust it, then you don't write another test to check, is that button doing its job as a button or not. Same as when you use bootstrap or other library components you don't write a test to check them. 

if you own the button in a project and let's say you are still not sure that it may get easily changed during a development process. Meaning code is easily accessible to other developers in your team, and they may try to play with it, then definitely you must have a test, as protection. Because today it's working, but tomorrow it may not.
comments.post_body:
A button has its own test cases. Now, if that button is used inside another component, is it necessary to test that same button while writing that component's tests also? 
Should we skip testing that button at that level? Or should we limit the test cases for that button?

P.S. No right or wrong answers here

#testing #writing #opinion #poll #webdevelopment

comments.context_summary: The commenter is discussing whether to test a button's functionality when it's used within another component, and is responding to the idea that child components should have their own unit tests for reusability and independence. The commenter is weighing the need to test the button at multiple levels versus limiting or skipping tests to avoid redundancy.

comments.comment_body: Brian very happy to hear about your new adventure. Sustainability tech is such an important field and I'm glad that you'll be a part of expanding it!
comments.post_body:
I’ve been delighting in the grief I’ve received for ending my last post on a cliffhanger. But, for anyone who’s been curious about my career change, I’m happy to announce that I’ve switched to Amazon’s Sustainability Tech organization. 

On the surface, this might not seem like a major change. I’ve already been a TPM at Amazon for more than a decade. 

However, this is the first time in my career I’ve worked on something that directly aligns with my values, as opposed to just being something I appreciate as a customer. Working on Prime Wardrobe’s expansion to third-party sellers was fun because I wear clothes (I’m sure you’re relieved to hear this), and launching that project made it easier for me as a customer to try clothes that I wanted. Working on sustainability hits at a deeper level. 

I’ll be curious to find out how this chapter in my career goes. It’s scarier than I expected working on something I directly care about - I’m not sure I’ll be able to go back. I’m also getting the impression that this is a space where people can burn out quickly. The danger is wanting to change the world NOW and then finding out it’s a longer slog than expected. 

This is another reason I’m grateful for my leave of absence. I’ll never be the world’s most patient person. (This is probably a good trait in a TPM.) However, I’m much better able to deal with short-term frustration for a long-term benefit than I would have been a few months ago.    

Most of all, I’m grateful to be at a company that allows me to keep trying new things sixteen years into my tenure, and work with some hella smart people along the way.

comments.comment_body: Jody where can I buy this?
comments.post_body:
Well, we did it.

This morning, as the sun was coming up, we completed the commercialization for SUNDAYS, a low carb and zero sugar Oreo I've been developing since early 2020.

In case anyone reading this wants to follow in my footsteps, here's the TL;DR on what it took;

32 months from start to finish

3 different co-packers in 2 different countries

3 separate R&D teams

5 full scale, production size trials

Hundreds of hours of bench work

Innumerable formula iterations

The total development cost was somewhere around $200,000

For one second today, it felt like I got to the finish line.

Then I remembered that I'm not at the finish line.

I'm at the starting line.

SUNDAYS will launch later this year.

Date is TBD.

Beyond grateful to everyone here that's been following along.

It's day zero.

Let's go baby!!
